Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set up a schedule at the beginning of each week, but life always got in the way. I'm 26, working part-time, and juggling a lot, so I ended up cramming most of my studying into random pockets of time. I'd watch videos late at night or skim through materials during lunch breaks. The 14 hours I managed to study each week were scattered everywhere, not really organized at all.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due. I rarely studied at the same time each week—it was more like deadline panic mode would kick in and suddenly I'd be glued to my laptop for hours. When there wasn't anything pressing, I'd drift and barely touch the course materials. I wish I could say I had a consistent routine, but that just wasn't my reality with everything else going on in my life.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline notification or realizing I had an assignment due soon that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about a discussion post or new content, and that would prompt me. Other times, I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so spotty. The gamified tasks actually helped—seeing those badges and progress bars gave me a small push to engage.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in became more routine for me. The gamified elements made it feel less like a chore and more like checking off achievements. I'd log in to see my progress and earn points, which felt oddly satisfying even when I was feeling low. But that routine faded when things got overwhelming, and I went back to only logging in when absolutely necessary.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly my lifeline. I'm a visual learner, so being able to watch and rewatch explanations really helped concepts stick. I also found the discussion boards useful when other students would explain things in different ways than the instructor. The gamified tasks made completing things feel more rewarding, even if the actual content was the same—it added a layer of motivation I desperately needed.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources and time, I couldn't print things out, and reading long documents on my phone or old laptop was exhausting. I also skipped a lot of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work without clear payoff. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade or a gamified reward, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate benefit—like it counted toward my grade or helped me understand something I was stuck on. The gamified elements helped here too; earning badges or seeing a progress bar fill up made even small tasks feel meaningful. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I just couldn't motivate myself to care about it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them or face consequences. But optional stuff? When you're stressed and sad and barely keeping up, optional just means 'skip this.' It's not that I didn't want to learn more; I just had to triage constantly. The gamified optional tasks were slightly better because at least there was some reward attached.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the video lectures and PDFs to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't always be near Wi-Fi. I'd watch videos on my phone during my commute or while waiting at work. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what I didn't understand. A lot of my actual learning happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours going over material on paper, making diagrams and visual summaries because that's how I learn best. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, but my real studying was happening in notebooks and on my phone's video player offline.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video once, download it, and then watch it three or four more times offline over the next few days. Same with some of the key readings—I'd save them and review them later when I had quiet time. The LMS probably showed I only accessed things once, but the reality was I was engaging with that content repeatedly, just not in ways the system could track.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. I've been dealing with a lot of sadness, and some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to care about coursework. Financial stress made it worse—worrying about bills while trying to focus on studying is brutal. My attendance dropped because sometimes I just couldn't show up, mentally or physically. Those were the hardest times.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue from work and stress made it hard to concentrate even when I did log into the LMS. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes just close the laptop instead of pushing through because I didn't have the energy. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, just surviving on limited resources—meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of attention I had left.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd have videos playing but I was completely zoned out, thinking about everything else going wrong in my life. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. The system probably registered me as active, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Being present and being engaged are two very different things.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, even when I knew they were important. Anxiety made it hard to start tasks because I was afraid of failing or not understanding. Sometimes the sadness just made everything feel pointless, so I'd put things off until the last possible moment. It wasn't laziness—it was more like emotional paralysis. The gamified deadlines helped a little, but they couldn't fix what was happening inside me.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore materials for weeks and then cram everything in the last few days before something was due. My LMS activity probably looked like these intense bursts followed by silence. It's not a healthy way to learn, but it's how I survived. The deadline pressure was stressful, but without it, I might not have engaged at all.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost and made me want to try harder. But when feedback was critical or I didn't do as well as I hoped, it sometimes made me withdraw more. With my mood already being low, negative feedback felt heavier than it probably should have. I tried to use it constructively, but emotionally it was hard not to take it personally.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me some framework to follow, which I needed. But the pace sometimes felt relentless—by the time I caught up on one thing, another was already due. The gamified elements added some structure that worked for me, but overall I felt like I was always playing catch-up rather than actually learning at a comfortable pace.
Q19: The visual elements and video content made engagement easier for me since that's how I learn best. The gamification also helped—it made logging in feel less like a burden. But the sheer volume of content and the expectation of regular attendance made things harder. With my life circumstances and mental state, a more flexible structure with self-paced options would have helped me engage more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but not finding it. They might watch videos repeatedly, browse through materials, participate in discussions—all trying to grasp concepts—but still struggle to produce quality work. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism, where someone engages a lot but can't bring themselves to submit because they're afraid it's not good enough.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My attendance was terrible and my LMS activity was sporadic, but I still managed decent grades because I was studying offline a lot. I'd download materials, take handwritten notes, use external resources—all invisible to the system. Someone looking at my logs might think I was barely trying, but I was working hard, just not in ways the LMS could see.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you were on a page. That's useful for seeing patterns. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the mental and emotional effort that goes into learning, and whether someone was actually engaged or just going through the motions. They can't capture understanding, struggle, or the real reasons behind someone's behavior.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being present, curious, and invested. For me, engagement happened in bursts when I could push past the sadness and stress. Real engagement is internal, and it doesn't always show up in activity logs or completion rates.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still trying to keep up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the anxiety before submitting assignments, or how the gamified elements actually gave me small moments of motivation during really dark times. It also wouldn't show that despite everything—the low attendance, the sadness, the stress—I genuinely wanted to learn and tried my best with what I had.
